theory.intro: Pythagoras' theorem also works in reverse. If the three sides of a triangle satisfy $a^2+b^2=h^2$, then the triangle must be right-angled. This lets us test a triangle, or a set of three numbers, without measuring any angle.
facts: **Converse of Pythagoras' theorem:** if the sides satisfy $a^2+b^2=h^2$, the triangle is **right-angled**.
If $a^2+b^2\neq h^2$, the triangle is **not** right-angled.
A **Pythagorean triad** is three **whole numbers** $(a,b,c)$ with $a^2+b^2=c^2$, where $c$ is the largest.
The hypotenuse $h$ (or the largest number $c$) is always the one squared on its own — test the largest side by itself.
Multiplying or dividing every number of a Pythagorean triad by the same whole number gives another Pythagorean triad.
steps: Identify the longest side as $h$ (or the largest number as $c$).
Add the squares of the two shorter sides: $a^2+b^2$.
Compare: if $a^2+b^2=h^2$ it is right-angled (a triad); if not, it is not.

foundation.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9]\coordinate (P) at (0,2.4);\coordinate (Q) at (2.6,2.4);\coordinate (R) at (2.6,0);\draw (P)--(Q) node[midway,above] {$8$};\draw (Q)--(R) node[midway,right] {$9$};\draw (R)--(P) node[midway,below left] {$12$};\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Is this triangle right-angled? Test using the converse of Pythagoras' theorem.
foundation.solution_text:
The longest side is $12$, so $h=12$.
$a^2+b^2$
$=8^2+9^2$
$=64+81$
$=145$

$h^2$
$=12^2$
$=144$

$145\neq144$, so the triangle is **not** right-angled.

foundation.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9]\coordinate (P) at (0,2.4);\coordinate (Q) at (2.6,2.4);\coordinate (R) at (2.6,0);\draw (P)--(Q) node[midway,above] {$8$};\draw (Q)--(R) node[midway,right] {$6$};\draw (R)--(P) node[midway,below left] {$10$};\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Is this triangle right-angled?

[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.4]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (B) at (3,0);\coordinate (C) at (0,1.3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$1.2$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,left] {$0.5$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above right] {$1.3$};\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Is this triangle right-angled?

foundation.solution_text:
The largest number is $9$, so let $c=9$.
$a^2+b^2$
$=5^2+7^2$
$=25+49$
$=74$

$c^2$
$=9^2$
$=81$

$74\neq81$, so $(5,7,9)$ is **not** a Pythagorean triad.

development.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (B) at (4,0);\coordinate (C) at (0,1.5);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$40$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,left] {$9$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above right] {$41$};\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Is this triangle right-angled? The diagram is not to scale.

development.solution_text:
The largest number is $26$, so let $c=26$ and use $10$ and $24$ as the shorter numbers.
$a^2+b^2$
$=10^2+24^2$
$=100+576$
$=676$

$c^2$
$=26^2$
$=676$

$676=676$, so $(24,26,10)$ **is** a Pythagorean triad.

development.question_text: $(8,15,17)$ is a Pythagorean triad. Is $(16,30,34)$ also a Pythagorean triad?
development.solution_text:
Each number of $(16,30,34)$ is a number of $(8,15,17)$ multiplied by $2$:
$8\times2=16$, $15\times2=30$, $17\times2=34$.
Multiplying every number of a triad by the same whole number gives another triad, so $(16,30,34)$ **is** a Pythagorean triad.

[/tikz]
Is this triangle right-angled? The diagram is not to scale.

mastery.question_text: $(8,15,17)$ is a Pythagorean triad. A student says $(9,16,18)$ must also be a triad because each number is a little bigger. Is $(9,16,18)$ a Pythagorean triad?
mastery.solution_text:
$(9,16,18)$ is not each number of $(8,15,17)$ multiplied by the same factor, so it must be tested directly.
The largest number is $18$, so let $c=18$.
$a^2+b^2$
$=9^2+16^2$
$=81+256$
$=337$

$c^2$
$=18^2$
$=324$

$337\neq324$, so $(9,16,18)$ is **not** a Pythagorean triad.
